articles.title: Roasts, Regrets, and Rain Checks: The Wildest White House Dinner Moments Ever
articles.summary: The White House Correspondents' Dinner is famous for its awkward, funny, and tense moments. This year, Donald Trump will attend for the first time as president. But the dinner has a history that goes way back. In 1983, Ronald Reagan skipped his jokes after U.S. soldiers were killed in Lebanon. In 2004, George W. Bush made fun of himself for not finding weapons in Iraq—but some veterans were furious. And in 2006, Stephen Colbert shocked everyone by roasting President Bush with unusual aggression. The dinner is where the president, journalists, and celebrities all hang out—and get roasted. It's Washington's most unpredictable night.

articles.title: The Carousel That Helped Change America: A Civil Rights Story on the National Mall
articles.summary: A historic carousel just reopened in Washington, D.C., after three years of restoration. But this merry-go-round isn't just about fun—it's a symbol of the Civil Rights Movement. In the 1960s, the carousel was at Gwynn Oak Amusement Park near Baltimore, which was segregated (whites only). After years of protests by Black and white activists, the park was finally integrated on August 28, 1963—the same day Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his "I Have a Dream" speech. The first Black child to ride was 11-month-old Sharon Langley. This week, 63-year-old Sharon rode again on a horse named "Freedom Rider." The carousel now stands in front of the Smithsonian's Arts and Industries Building, a monument to the struggle for equality.

articles.title: Alcaraz's French Open Threepeat Dream Crushed by Wrist Injury
articles.summary: Carlos Alcaraz, the two-time defending French Open champion, will miss this year's tournament due to a right wrist injury. The 22-year-old Spaniard sustained the injury during the Barcelona Open and withdrew from the Madrid Masters. Tests revealed he needs to rest, ruling him out of the Italian Open and Roland Garros. Alcaraz became the youngest man to complete the career Grand Slam by winning the Australian Open in January. He lost the world number one spot to Jannik Sinner after the Monte Carlo Masters final. Sinner called the news "sad" and hopes Alcaraz returns for Wimbledon. Alcaraz said he'd rather miss tournaments than risk long-term damage. This is only the second Grand Slam he's missed since 2021.
